quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Magnetic force on a charge"?

options.text: Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
F = qv × B; force is perpendicular to both v and B; magnitude F = qvB sin θ; does no work (changes direction, not speed)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Magnetic force on a charge" is defined as: F = qv × B; force is perpendicular to both v and B; magnitude F = qvB sin θ; does no work (changes direction, not speed). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Magnetic force on a wire"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N
High-voltage transmission minimizes I²R losses: P_loss = I²R = P²R/V²; higher V → lower I → much lower losses
Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B

quiz_questions.explanation: "Magnetic force on a wire" is defined as: F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Right-hand rule (force)"?

options.text: When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each
Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)
Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge

quiz_questions.explanation: "Right-hand rule (force)" is defined as: Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Magnetic field of a wire"?

options.text: r = mv/(qB); radius of circular path depends on mass, speed, and charge-to-field ratio; the basis for particle accelerators
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light

quiz_questions.explanation: "Magnetic field of a wire" is defined as: B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Solenoid field"?

options.text: B = μ₀nI inside a solenoid (n = turns/length); uniform field inside, nearly zero outside; like a bar magnet
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns
1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Solenoid field" is defined as: B = μ₀nI inside a solenoid (n = turns/length); uniform field inside, nearly zero outside; like a bar magnet. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = qv × B; force is perpendicular to both v and B; magnitude F = qvB sin θ; does no work (changes direction, not speed)"?

options.text: Wien's displacement law
Beta-minus decay
Magnetic force on a charge
Kirchhoff's loop rule

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Magnetic force on a charge".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B"?

options.text: Index of refraction
Magnetic force on a wire
Second Law of Thermodynamics
Rutherford model

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Magnetic force on a wire".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge"?

options.text: Magnetic domains
Charge conservation
Right-hand rule (force)
Beats

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Right-hand rule (force)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire"?

options.text: Rydberg formula
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Magnetic field of a wire
Electric field

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Magnetic field of a wire".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "B = μ₀nI inside a solenoid (n = turns/length); uniform field inside, nearly zero outside; like a bar magnet"?

options.text: Electric potential (voltage)
Standing waves
Solenoid field
Snell's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Solenoid field".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Magnetism is accurate?

options.text: Magnetism studies magnetic fields and the forces they exert on moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Magnetism?

options.text: Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Moving charges create magnetic fields, and magnetic fields exert forces on moving charges: F = qv × B (the Lorentz force).
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Magnetism?

options.text: The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.
The Rydberg formula for hydrogen: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
For a current-carrying wire in a field: F = IL × B.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Magnetism?

options.text: Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).
The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.
Right-hand rules determine the direction of magnetic force and of fields produced by currents.
Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Magnetism?

options.text: Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Magnetic field lines form closed loops (no magnetic monopoles exist).
Sign conventions must be applied consistently for accurate results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Magnetism as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Magnetism?

options.text: MRI machines use strong magnetic fields (1.5-7 Tesla) to align hydrogen nuclei in the body; radio pulses flip the alignment and the return signal creates detailed tissue images
Quantum computing uses superposition and entanglement to process information exponentially faster for certain problems (factoring, simulation, optimization)
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Magnetism?

options.text: Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetism.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Magnetism applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Hydraulic systems use Pascal's principle: a small force on a small piston creates a large force on a large piston (F₂ = F₁ · A₂/A₁), powering car brakes and industrial presses
Earth's magnetic field (25-65 μT) is generated by convection currents in the liquid iron outer core (geodynamo); it deflects solar wind and protects life from harmful radiation
Radio telescope arrays (like the VLA) combine signals from many dishes using interference principles to achieve resolution equivalent to a single dish spanning kilometers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Magnetism?

options.text: MRI machines use strong magnetic fields (1.5-7 Tesla) to align hydrogen nuclei in the body; radio pulses flip the alignment and the return signal creates detailed tissue images
Airplane wing lift: the curved upper surface forces air to move faster (lower pressure above) than the flat lower surface (higher pressure below), creating net upward force via Bernoulli's principle
Musical instrument design: the length and shape of brass instruments determine which harmonics are produced; a bugle plays only natural harmonics of its tube length
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Magnetism in practice?

options.text: Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
Medical X-rays: high-energy photons penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone, creating shadow images for diagnosing fractures and other conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Magnetism.
